name: Fournier's Gangrene
signs:
Perineal/scrotal pain and swelling
Rapid progression
Crepitus (subcutaneous gas)
Fever and sepsis
Erythema with dusky discoloration
Foul-smelling discharge
Systemic toxicity out of proportion to exam
treatment:
1. Emergent surgical debridement (most important)
2. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
3. Aggressive fluid resuscitation
4. Repeat debridement q24-48h until clean margins
5. ICU admission
6. Wound VAC after debridement
7. Reconstructive surgery after infection controlled
meds:
Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h
Clindamycin 900mg IV q8h (toxin suppression)
Meropenem 1g IV q8h (alternative)
IV fluids aggressive resuscitation
Vasopressors as needed
ddx: Scrotal abscess, Epididymitis, Cellulitis, Incarcerated hernia, Testicular torsion, Necrotizing fasciitis (other sites)
labs:
CBC (leukocytosis)
BMP (renal function, acidosis)
Lactate elevated
Blood cultures
CT pelvis (gas tracking in soft tissues)
Wound cultures (polymicrobial)
Coagulation studies
ProCalcitonin
notes:
Polymicrobial necrotizing fasciitis of perineum/genitalia
Risk factors: diabetes (most common), immunosuppression, alcoholism, obesity
Mortality 20-40% even with treatment
LFINSI score predicts severity and mortality
Often polymicrobial: E. coli, Bacteroides, Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, Clostridium
Surgical debridement is definitive - antibiotics alone are insufficient
Fecal diversion (colostomy) may be needed if perianal source
description: A rapidly progressive, life-threatening necrotizing infection of the perineal and genital area requiring emergency surgical debridement and broad-spectrum antibiotics.

name: Epididymitis
signs:
Gradual onset scrotal pain
Swollen, tender epididymis
Fever
Dysuria and urinary frequency
Positive Prehn sign (relief with elevation)
Cremaster reflex intact
Reactive hydrocele
treatment:
1. Antibiotics based on likely pathogen
2. Scrotal elevation and support
3. NSAIDs and ice for pain
4. Rest
5. STI testing and partner treatment if sexually transmitted
6. Urology follow-up if no improvement in 48-72h
meds:
Ceftriaxone 500mg IM x1 + doxycycline 100mg BID x10 days (STI-related, age <35)
Levofloxacin 500mg daily x10 days (non-STI, age >35, enteric organisms)
Ibuprofen 600mg TID
Acetaminophen 1g QID
Scrotal support (jockstrap)
ddx: Testicular torsion (emergency), Torsion of appendix testis, Orchitis, Inguinal hernia, Hydrocele, Testicular tumor, Trauma
labs:
Urinalysis and urine culture
STI testing: GC/chlamydia NAAT
CBC
Color Doppler ultrasound (increased blood flow to epididymis, vs absent in torsion)
Urethral swab if discharge
notes:
Age <35: usually sexually transmitted (N. gonorrhoeae, C. trachomatis)
Age >35: usually enteric organisms (E. coli) from UTI or BPH
Key differentiator from torsion: gradual onset, positive Prehn sign, intact cremaster reflex, pyuria
If uncertain between torsion and epididymitis: surgical exploration (don't miss torsion)
Complications: abscess, chronic pain, infertility
Pediatric epididymitis: usually non-infectious, may be post-viral
description: Inflammation of the epididymis, the coiled tube behind the testicle, usually caused by bacterial infection from sexually transmitted organisms in younger men or urinary bacteria in older men.

name: Urinary Retention
signs:
Inability to void
Suprapubic fullness and discomfort
Overflow incontinence
Palpable/percussible bladder
Lower abdominal distension
Agitation in elderly/demented patients
Renal failure if bilateral obstruction
treatment:
1. Immediate bladder catheterization (Foley or intermittent)
2. Decompress gradually (controversial - drain freely per recent evidence)
3. Identify and treat underlying cause
4. Alpha-blocker trial before catheter removal
5. Trial of void after 1-3 days of catheter drainage
6. Clean intermittent catheterization if chronic
7. Surgery for BPH if refractory
meds:
Tamsulosin 0.4mg daily (start before catheter removal trial)
Finasteride 5mg daily (large prostate)
Bethanechol 10-50mg TID (detrusor underactivity, limited evidence)
Oxybutynin AVOID (worsens retention)
Discontinue anticholinergics, antihistamines, decongestants
ddx: BPH, Urethral stricture, Prostate cancer, Neurogenic bladder, Medication-induced, Constipation/fecal impaction, Cauda equina syndrome, Post-operative
labs:
Post-void residual (>200mL significant, >500mL = retention)
BMP (creatinine - renal function)
Urinalysis and culture
PSA
Renal ultrasound (hydronephrosis)
Cystoscopy if stricture suspected
Urodynamic studies (chronic/neurogenic)
notes:
Acute: sudden inability to void, painful, requires immediate catheterization
Chronic: incomplete emptying, overflow incontinence, painless
Common medications causing retention: anticholinergics, antihistamines, opioids, TCAs, alpha-agonists (pseudoephedrine)
Post-operative retention: common after surgery, anesthesia, especially with opioids
Cauda equina: urinary retention + saddle anesthesia + bilateral leg symptoms → emergent MRI
Decompression cystitis: hematuria after drainage (benign, self-limited)
Post-obstructive diuresis: monitor UO and replace fluids if >200mL/hr
description: The inability to completely empty the bladder, which can occur suddenly (acute) or develop gradually (chronic), most commonly caused by prostate enlargement in older men.

name: Renal Cell Carcinoma
signs:
Classic triad (10%): hematuria, flank pain, palpable mass
Most found incidentally on imaging
Weight loss
Fever of unknown origin
Varicocele (left-sided, doesn't decompress when supine)
Paraneoplastic syndromes: polycythemia, hypercalcemia, hypertension
Stauffer syndrome (hepatic dysfunction)
treatment:
1. Localized: partial or radical nephrectomy
2. Small tumors (<4cm): active surveillance or ablation option
3. Metastatic: systemic therapy (immunotherapy + TKI)
4. Cytoreductive nephrectomy in select metastatic cases
5. Surveillance protocol after resection
6. Radiation for bone/brain metastases
meds:
Pembrolizumab 200mg IV q3wk + axitinib 5mg BID (metastatic, first-line)
Nivolumab 3mg/kg + ipilimumab 1mg/kg IV (metastatic, first-line)
Sunitinib 50mg daily (4wk on/2wk off)
Cabozantinib 60mg daily
Pazopanib 800mg daily
Everolimus 10mg daily (second-line)
Radiation for symptomatic metastases
ddx: Renal cyst, Angiomyolipoma, Oncocytoma, Transitional cell carcinoma, Renal abscess, Adrenal mass, Retroperitoneal sarcoma
labs:
CT abdomen with and without contrast (enhancing renal mass)
Renal mass biopsy (if diagnosis uncertain)
CBC (polycythemia or anemia)
BMP (calcium, creatinine)
LFTs
LDH
Chest CT (staging)
Brain MRI and bone scan (if symptomatic)
Urinalysis
notes:
Clear cell: most common subtype (75%), VHL pathway, responds to TKI/immunotherapy
Papillary and chromophobe: less common, different biology
Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome: bilateral/multifocal RCC, hemangioblastomas, pheochromocytoma
RCC can metastasize to unusual sites: bone, lung, brain, contralateral kidney
RCC is classically resistant to traditional chemotherapy and radiation
Spontaneous regression of metastases: rare but reported (immune-mediated)
IVC tumor thrombus: surgical challenge, may extend to right atrium
description: The most common type of kidney cancer in adults, often found incidentally on imaging, arising from the cells lining the kidney tubules and frequently associated with paraneoplastic syndromes.

name: Prostate Cancer
signs:
Often asymptomatic (detected by PSA screening)
Urinary symptoms (frequency, hesitancy, nocturia)
Bone pain (metastatic)
Hard, irregular nodule on DRE
Hematuria (rare)
Erectile dysfunction
Weight loss (advanced)
treatment:
1. Low-risk: active surveillance (preferred for Gleason <=6)
2. Intermediate-risk: radical prostatectomy or radiation + ADT
3. High-risk: radiation + long-term ADT (2-3 years)
4. Metastatic hormone-sensitive: ADT + docetaxel or novel hormonal agent
5. Castration-resistant: enzalutamide, abiraterone, docetaxel, radium-223
6. Genetic testing for BRCA (PARP inhibitor eligibility)
meds:
Leuprolide 22.5mg IM q3 months (LHRH agonist, ADT)
Degarelix 240mg SC then 80mg monthly (GnRH antagonist)
Enzalutamide 160mg daily
Abiraterone 1000mg daily + prednisone 5mg BID
Docetaxel 75mg/m2 IV q3wk
Olaparib 300mg BID (BRCA-mutated)
Radium-223 (bone metastases)
Zoledronic acid 4mg IV q3-4wk (bone protection)
ddx: BPH, Prostatitis, Bladder cancer, UTI, Urethral stricture, Neurogenic bladder
labs:
PSA (elevated, but not specific)
DRE
Transrectal ultrasound-guided biopsy or MRI-fusion biopsy
Multiparametric prostate MRI (PI-RADS scoring)
Gleason score/Grade Group (biopsy)
Bone scan (if PSA >20 or high-risk)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (staging)
Testosterone level
Genetic testing: BRCA1/2, ATM, MSI
notes:
Most common cancer in men, second leading cause of cancer death
Screening: shared decision-making for PSA starting age 50 (or 40-45 if high-risk: Black race, family history)
Gleason score: sum of two most common patterns (3+3=6 favorable, 4+3=7 intermediate, 4+5=9 high-risk)
Active surveillance for low-risk: PSA q6mo, repeat biopsy q1-2 years
ADT side effects: hot flashes, osteoporosis, metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular risk, cognitive changes
Tumor flare with LHRH agonists: give antiandrogen cover for first 2 weeks
PSMA-PET: most sensitive imaging for recurrent/metastatic disease
description: Cancer of the prostate gland, the most common cancer in men, ranging from slow-growing tumors suitable for monitoring to aggressive disease requiring multimodal treatment.

name: Bladder Cancer
signs:
Painless gross hematuria (most common presenting symptom)
Frequency and urgency
Dysuria
Pelvic pain (advanced)
Weight loss
Flank pain (ureteral obstruction)
Lower extremity edema (pelvic lymphadenopathy)
treatment:
1. Non-muscle invasive (Ta, T1, CIS): TURBT + intravesical therapy
2. BCG intravesical instillation (standard for CIS and high-risk NMIBC)
3. Muscle-invasive (T2+): neoadjuvant cisplatin chemo then radical cystectomy
4. Metastatic: cisplatin-based chemo + immunotherapy
5. Trimodal therapy (TURBT + chemo + radiation) for bladder preservation
6. Urine diversion after cystectomy (ileal conduit or neobladder)
meds:
Intravesical BCG weekly x6 then maintenance
Intravesical gemcitabine 2g (alternative to BCG)
Cisplatin + gemcitabine (neoadjuvant/metastatic)
Pembrolizumab 200mg IV q3wk (BCG-unresponsive NMIBC or metastatic)
Avelumab 800mg IV q2wk (maintenance after chemo)
Enfortumab vedotin (metastatic, post-chemo)
Erdafitinib 8mg daily (FGFR-altered)
ddx: UTI, Renal calculi, Glomerulonephritis, Renal cell carcinoma, Prostate cancer (hematuria), BPH, Urothelial carcinoma of upper tract, Radiation cystitis
labs:
Urinalysis (hematuria)
Urine cytology
Cystoscopy with biopsy (diagnostic, gold standard)
CT urogram (upper tract evaluation)
CBC, BMP
LFTs
Chest CT (staging)
PET-CT (metastatic workup)
MRI pelvis (local staging)
Urine FISH or molecular markers
notes:
Urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma: 90% of bladder cancers
Risk factors: smoking (#1), occupational exposures (aromatic amines, dyes, rubber), cyclophosphamide, radiation
Non-muscle invasive: 75% of presentations, but 50-70% recur
BCG: live attenuated Mycobacterium bovis, most effective intravesical therapy for NMIBC
BCG shortage: alternative intravesical agents (gemcitabine + docetaxel)
Smoking cessation: reduces recurrence risk by 40%
CIS (carcinoma in situ): flat, high-grade, aggressive - BCG is standard
description: Cancer of the bladder lining, most commonly presenting as painless blood in the urine, strongly associated with smoking, and treated based on whether it has invaded the bladder muscle.

name: Cystic Fibrosis
signs:
Chronic productive cough
Recurrent pulmonary infections (Pseudomonas)
Failure to thrive
Steatorrhea (fatty, foul-smelling stools)
Digital clubbing
Nasal polyps
Meconium ileus (neonates)
Male infertility
Salty-tasting skin
treatment:
1. Airway clearance techniques (chest PT, vest therapy, PEP devices)
2. Inhaled mucolytics (dornase alfa, hypertonic saline)
3. CFTR modulators (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor - Trikafta)
4. Pancreatic enzyme replacement
5. Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K)
6. Aggressive treatment of pulmonary exacerbations
7. Lung transplant for end-stage disease
meds:
Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (Trikafta) daily (eligible mutations)
Dornase alfa 2.5mg nebulized daily
Hypertonic saline 7% nebulized BID
Albuterol nebulized before airway clearance
Pancrelipase (Creon) with meals and snacks
Azithromycin 500mg MWF (anti-inflammatory)
Tobramycin inhaled 300mg BID alternating months
Vitamins A, D, E, K supplementation
ddx: Primary ciliary dyskinesia, Bronchiectasis (other causes), Asthma, Immunodeficiency, Celiac disease, Shwachman-Diamond syndrome
labs:
Newborn screening (IRT + CFTR mutations)
Sweat chloride test (>=60 mmol/L diagnostic)
CFTR genotyping (>2000 mutations, F508del most common)
Sputum culture (Pseudomonas, S. aureus, B. cepacia)
PFTs (FEV1 monitoring)
Fecal elastase (pancreatic insufficiency)
Fat-soluble vitamin levels
OGTT annually after age 10 (CFRD screening)
Liver function tests
Chest CT
notes:
Autosomal recessive, CFTR gene (chromosome 7), F508del most common mutation (~70%)
Trikafta: revolutionary - eligible for ~90% of CF patients, dramatically improves lung function and outcomes
Median survival now >50 years (was ~30 in 2000s, improving rapidly with CFTR modulators)
Pseudomonas acquisition: chronic infection worsens prognosis
Burkholderia cepacia: poor prognosis, relative contraindication to transplant
CFRD (CF-related diabetes): unique pathophysiology, treat with insulin (not metformin)
Meconium ileus at birth: pathognomonic for CF
description: A genetic disorder affecting the lungs and digestive system, caused by mutations in the CFTR gene that produce thick, sticky mucus leading to chronic lung infections and pancreatic insufficiency.

name: Febrile Seizure
signs:
Generalized tonic-clonic seizure
Associated with fever (usually >100.4°F/38°C)
Age 6 months - 5 years
Seizure typically lasts <15 minutes
Post-ictal drowsiness
Rapid return to baseline
No focal neurological deficits
treatment:
1. Acute seizure: ensure safety, position on side
2. Benzodiazepine if seizure >5 minutes
3. Antipyretics for comfort (do NOT prevent febrile seizures)
4. Identify and treat source of fever
5. Reassurance to parents (benign prognosis)
6. No routine anti-seizure medication for simple febrile seizures
7. LP only if clinical concern for meningitis
meds:
Diazepam 0.5mg/kg rectal (acute seizure >5min)
Midazolam 0.2mg/kg intranasal or buccal
Acetaminophen 15mg/kg q4h PRN (comfort, does NOT prevent recurrence)
Ibuprofen 10mg/kg q6h PRN (comfort)
Antibiotics only if bacterial source identified
Daily anticonvulsants NOT recommended for simple FS
ddx: Meningitis/encephalitis, Epilepsy, Shaking/rigors, Breath-holding spell, Electrolyte disturbance, Intracranial pathology, Non-accidental trauma
labs:
Simple febrile seizure: no routine workup needed
Focus on fever source: UA, CXR, blood culture as indicated
LP: consider if <12 months (especially if unvaccinated), meningeal signs, complex FS, or pretreated with antibiotics
EEG NOT recommended for simple FS
BMP only if clinically indicated
CBC if source unclear
notes:
Simple FS: generalized, <15 min, once in 24h, age 6mo-5yr, normal development
Complex FS: focal, >15 min, recurs within 24h, or abnormal neuro exam
Recurrence risk: ~30% overall (50% if first FS <12 months)
Epilepsy risk after simple FS: 1-2% (same as general population)
Complex FS: slightly higher epilepsy risk but still low
Antipyretics do NOT prevent febrile seizures (multiple RCTs)
Parent education is crucial: most parents fear their child is dying during seizure
description: A seizure occurring in a young child triggered by fever, typically benign and self-limiting, not caused by epilepsy and not requiring long-term anti-seizure medication.

name: Neonatal Jaundice
signs:
Yellow discoloration of skin and sclerae
Progresses cephalocaudally (head → trunk → extremities)
Poor feeding
Lethargy
High-pitched cry (kernicterus)
Hypotonia then hypertonia (kernicterus)
Typically appears after 24h of life (physiologic)
treatment:
1. Phototherapy (first-line based on bilirubin nomogram)
2. Intensive phototherapy for rapid rise or high levels
3. Exchange transfusion for severe cases or failed phototherapy
4. Frequent feeding (breast milk or formula) q2-3h
5. Address underlying cause (hemolysis, infection)
6. IV fluids if dehydrated
7. Monitor bilirubin q4-24h based on level and trajectory
meds:
Phototherapy (blue-green light 430-490nm wavelength)
IVIG 0.5-1g/kg (isoimmune hemolytic disease)
IV fluids D10W if dehydrated
Phenobarbital (rarely, Crigler-Najjar)
Exchange transfusion: double-volume (170mL/kg)
No need for routine supplementation if breastfeeding well
ddx: Physiologic jaundice, Breast milk jaundice, ABO/Rh incompatibility, G6PD deficiency, Hereditary spherocytosis, Sepsis, Biliary atresia, Hypothyroidism, Cephalohematoma reabsorption, Crigler-Najjar
labs:
Total and direct bilirubin (plot on Bhutani nomogram)
Blood type and Coombs test (mother and infant)
CBC with reticulocyte count and peripheral smear
G6PD level
Transcutaneous bilirubinometry (screening)
Direct bilirubin (if elevated: think biliary atresia, TORCH)
Albumin level
TSH (newborn screen)
notes:
Physiologic: appears day 2-3, peaks day 3-5, resolves by 2 weeks
Pathologic: <24h, rate >5mg/dL/day, >95th percentile on nomogram, direct >1mg/dL or >20% of total
Kernicterus: bilirubin encephalopathy, acute (hypotonia, poor feeding) → chronic (choreoathetoid CP, hearing loss, dental enamel dysplasia)
Breast milk jaundice: prolonged unconjugated, peaks week 2, benign
Breastfeeding jaundice: inadequate intake, dehydration, first week
Bhutani nomogram: hour-specific bilirubin risk zones guide management
AAP phototherapy thresholds vary by gestational age and risk factors
description: Yellowing of a newborn's skin and eyes caused by elevated bilirubin levels, usually physiologic and harmless but requiring monitoring and treatment with phototherapy in severe cases to prevent brain damage.

name: Neonatal Sepsis
signs:
Temperature instability (fever or hypothermia)
Poor feeding
Lethargy or irritability
Respiratory distress (tachypnea, grunting, apnea)
Tachycardia or bradycardia
Hypotension
Jaundice
Abdominal distension
Seizures
Poor perfusion (mottling, delayed cap refill)
treatment:
1. Empiric antibiotics immediately after cultures obtained
2. Early-onset (<72h): ampicillin + gentamicin
3. Late-onset (>72h): vancomycin + gentamicin (or cefepime)
4. Respiratory support as needed
5. IV fluids and vasopressors for shock
6. Duration: 7-21 days depending on organism and site
7. LP when clinically stable
meds:
Ampicillin 50mg/kg IV q8-12h (early-onset)
Gentamicin 4-5mg/kg IV q24-48h (based on GA)
Vancomycin 15mg/kg IV q8-12h (late-onset)
Cefotaxime 50mg/kg IV q8h (meningitis doses)
Acyclovir 20mg/kg IV q8h (if HSV suspected)
Fluconazole (Candida prophylaxis in ELBW)
Dopamine/epinephrine for refractory shock
ddx: Transient tachypnea of newborn, RDS, Congenital heart disease, Inborn errors of metabolism, NEC, Congenital viral infection (TORCH), Maternal chorioamnionitis without neonatal infection
labs:
Blood culture (before antibiotics)
CBC with differential (immature:total neutrophil ratio)
CRP (serial, more useful for monitoring)
ProCalcitonin (rising)
UA and urine culture (late-onset)
LP and CSF analysis (cell count, protein, glucose, culture)
Chest XR if respiratory symptoms
BMP, blood gas
HSV PCR if vesicles or maternal history
notes:
Early-onset (<72h): GBS (#1), E. coli (#2), Listeria. Acquired during birth
Late-onset (>72h): CoNS (#1 in NICU), S. aureus, gram-negatives, Candida
GBS prophylaxis: intrapartum penicillin G if maternal GBS+ or risk factors
Rule of 2: if blood culture negative at 36-48h AND well-appearing, can stop antibiotics
HSV: consider if maternal genital herpes, vesicles, seizures, LFT abnormalities, CSF pleocytosis
NEC: bloody stools + abdominal distension + pneumatosis intestinalis
Mortality: 10-25% (higher in preterm, gram-negative, meningitis)
description: A serious bloodstream infection in newborns occurring within the first month of life, caused by bacteria acquired before, during, or after birth, requiring immediate antibiotic treatment.

name: Pertussis
signs:
Catarrhal phase (1-2 weeks): rhinorrhea, mild cough, low-grade fever
Paroxysmal phase (2-8 weeks): severe coughing fits with inspiratory whoop
Post-tussive vomiting
Cyanosis during paroxysms
Apnea (infants - may be only sign)
Subconjunctival hemorrhages from coughing
Convalescent phase: gradual improvement
treatment:
1. Macrolide antibiotics (reduce transmission, may shorten illness if given early)
2. Supportive care: oxygen, suctioning, hydration
3. Hospitalize infants <6 months
4. Post-exposure prophylaxis for close contacts
5. Droplet precautions until 5 days of antibiotics
6. DTaP vaccination for prevention
meds:
Azithromycin 10mg/kg day 1 then 5mg/kg days 2-5 (preferred in infants <1 month)
Erythromycin 40mg/kg/day divided QID x14 days
TMP-SMX (macrolide-intolerant)
Supplemental oxygen PRN
IV fluids if unable to feed
Post-exposure prophylaxis: same antibiotics for household contacts
ddx: Viral URI, Bronchiolitis, Croup, Foreign body aspiration, Chlamydia pneumonia (infants), Mycoplasma, Tuberculosis, Reactive airway disease
labs:
Nasopharyngeal PCR (most sensitive, test of choice)
Nasopharyngeal culture on Regan-Lowe or Bordet-Gengou media (gold standard but slow)
CBC: marked lymphocytosis (>10,000-100,000 in infants - associated with mortality)
CXR: perihilar infiltrates or shaggy heart border
Pertussis serology (epidemiologic, not acute diagnosis)
notes:
Bordetella pertussis, 100-day cough
Infants <6 months: highest morbidity/mortality, may not have classic whoop
Leukocytosis with lymphocytosis: hallmark in infants, WBC >100K associated with mortality
Vaccination: DTaP (children), Tdap (adolescents/adults), Tdap in pregnancy (27-36 weeks each pregnancy)
Immunity wanes 5-10 years after vaccination → adolescent/adult cases increasing
Cocooning strategy: vaccinate family members around newborn
Post-exposure prophylaxis within 21 days for household contacts regardless of vaccination status
description: A highly contagious bacterial respiratory infection known as 'whooping cough,' causing severe coughing fits that can be life-threatening in young infants who may not yet be fully vaccinated.

name: Measles
signs:
Prodrome: high fever (often >104°F), cough, coryza, conjunctivitis (3 C's)
Koplik spots (white spots on buccal mucosa - pathognomonic)
Maculopapular rash starting on face, spreads downward
Rash appears ~day 4 of illness
Fever peaks with rash onset then improves
Lymphadenopathy
Pharyngitis
treatment:
1. Supportive care (fluids, antipyretics, rest)
2. Vitamin A supplementation (reduces mortality and complications)
3. Airborne isolation until 4 days after rash onset
4. Report to public health
5. Post-exposure prophylaxis: MMR vaccine within 72h or immunoglobulin within 6 days
6. Treat complications (pneumonia, encephalitis)
meds:
Vitamin A: 200,000 IU PO x2 days (children >12 months)
Vitamin A: 100,000 IU (6-12 months), 50,000 IU (<6 months)
Acetaminophen for fever
IV fluids if dehydrated
Antibiotics only for bacterial superinfection
MMR vaccine for post-exposure prophylaxis (within 72h of exposure)
ddx: Rubella, Roseola, Scarlet fever, Kawasaki disease, Drug eruption, Mononucleosis, Parvovirus B19, Rocky Mountain spotted fever
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (in endemic settings)
Measles IgM antibody (positive 3 days after rash)
Measles RNA PCR (nasopharyngeal, throat, urine)
CBC (leukopenia, lymphopenia)
CXR if pneumonia suspected
Viral culture (reference labs)
notes:
Highly contagious: R0 = 12-18, airborne transmission, infectious 4 days before to 4 days after rash
Complications: pneumonia (#1 cause of death), encephalitis (1/1000), SSPE (rare, years later)
Vaccination: MMR at 12-15 months and 4-6 years (97% effective after 2 doses)
Immunocompromised: giant cell pneumonia, can be fatal, no rash (modified measles)
Reportable disease - immediate notification to public health
Vitamin A deficiency increases measles mortality - supplement all cases
SSPE (subacute sclerosing panencephalitis): progressive neurodegeneration, occurs 7-10 years post-measles
description: A highly contagious viral infection causing high fever, cough, and a characteristic spreading rash, preventable by vaccination but potentially causing serious complications including pneumonia and brain inflammation.

name: Scarlet Fever
signs:
Sandpaper-like erythematous rash
Strawberry tongue (white then red)
Pastia lines (linear petechiae in skin folds)
Circumoral pallor
Fever
Sore throat
Cervical lymphadenopathy
Desquamation of fingertips (late, 1-2 weeks)
treatment:
1. Penicillin or amoxicillin (first-line)
2. Treat for 10 days to prevent rheumatic fever
3. Supportive care: fluids, antipyretics, soft diet
4. May return to school 24h after starting antibiotics
5. Monitor for complications
meds:
Amoxicillin 50mg/kg/day divided BID x10 days
Penicillin V 250mg BID-TID x10 days (weight-based)
Benzathine penicillin G 600,000-1.2M units IM x1
Azithromycin 12mg/kg day 1 then 6mg/kg days 2-5 (penicillin allergy)
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for fever and pain
ddx: Viral exanthem, Kawasaki disease, Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome, Drug eruption, Toxic shock syndrome, Measles, Mononucleosis
labs:
Rapid strep antigen test (throat swab)
Throat culture (gold standard if rapid test negative)
ASO titer and anti-DNase B (if post-strep complications suspected)
CBC
Urinalysis (post-strep glomerulonephritis screening 1-2 weeks after)
notes:
Caused by Group A Streptococcus (GAS) producing erythrogenic toxin
Typically ages 5-15 years
Complication prevention: full antibiotic course prevents acute rheumatic fever (NOT PSGN)
Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis: 1-3 weeks after, hematuria, not prevented by antibiotics
Rheumatic fever: 2-4 weeks after, prevented by timely antibiotic treatment
Sandpaper rash: characteristic texture, blanches with pressure
Pastia lines persist after rash fades - helpful late diagnostic clue
description: A bacterial illness caused by Group A Streptococcus that produces a distinctive sandpaper-textured rash, strawberry tongue, and fever, treated with antibiotics to prevent serious complications.

name: Pancreatic Cancer
signs:
Painless jaundice (head of pancreas)
Weight loss
Epigastric pain radiating to back
New-onset diabetes
Steatorrhea
Courvoisier sign (palpable non-tender gallbladder)
Migratory thrombophlebitis (Trousseau syndrome)
Depression
Anorexia
treatment:
1. Surgical resection if resectable (only 15-20% at diagnosis)
2. Whipple procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy) for head tumors
3. Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (borderline resectable)
4. Adjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX after resection
5. Palliative chemotherapy for metastatic disease
6. Biliary stenting for obstructive jaundice
7. Pain management (celiac plexus block)
8. Palliative care integration
meds:
Modified FOLFIRINOX (5-FU, leucovorin, irinotecan, oxaliplatin)
Gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel (alternative first-line)
Gemcitabine monotherapy (poor performance status)
Olaparib (BRCA-mutated, maintenance)
Pembrolizumab (MSI-H, rare)
Pancreatic enzyme replacement (exocrine insufficiency)
Celiac plexus block or neurolysis for pain
ddx: Cholangiocarcinoma, Ampullary carcinoma, Chronic pancreatitis, Autoimmune pancreatitis, Pancreatic cyst/IPMN, CBD stone, Gastric cancer
labs:
CT abdomen with pancreatic protocol (triple-phase)
CA 19-9 (tumor marker, not screening)
EUS with FNA (tissue diagnosis, staging)
MRI/MRCP (biliary anatomy)
CBC, BMP, LFTs (obstructive pattern)
Bilirubin (elevated with head tumors)
Chest CT (staging)
PET-CT (metastatic workup)
Genetic testing: BRCA1/2, PALB2, MLH1, MSH2
notes:
5-year survival: ~10% overall (worst of common cancers)
Adenocarcinoma: 85% of pancreatic cancers, head location most common (60-70%)
Resectability: borderline if involves SMA/celiac ≤180°, unresectable if >180° or distant mets
CA 19-9: useful for monitoring treatment response, not screening (false-positive in jaundice)
Risk factors: smoking, chronic pancreatitis, family history, obesity, new-onset diabetes after 50
Autoimmune pancreatitis (IgG4): important mimic, responds to steroids
Whipple mortality: <5% at high-volume centers, but high morbidity
description: A highly aggressive cancer of the pancreas with poor prognosis, often diagnosed late due to vague symptoms, with surgical removal possible in only a minority of cases.

name: Hepatocellular Carcinoma
signs:
Often asymptomatic (found on screening in cirrhotics)
RUQ pain or fullness
Weight loss
Hepatomegaly
Ascites (new or worsening)
Jaundice
Paraneoplastic: erythrocytosis, hypercalcemia, hypoglycemia
Bloody ascites
Hepatic bruit
treatment:
1. Surgical resection (non-cirrhotic or well-compensated cirrhosis)
2. Liver transplant (Milan criteria: single ≤5cm or up to 3 lesions each ≤3cm)
3. Locoregional: RFA, TACE, Y-90 radioembolization
4. Systemic: atezolizumab + bevacizumab (first-line advanced)
5. Sorafenib or lenvatinib (alternative first-line)
6. Curative intent transplant cures both cancer and cirrhosis
meds:
Atezolizumab 1200mg + bevacizumab 15mg/kg IV q3wk (first-line advanced)
Sorafenib 400mg BID
Lenvatinib 8-12mg daily
Cabozantinib 60mg daily (second-line)
Regorafenib 160mg daily (second-line after sorafenib)
Nivolumab + ipilimumab (second-line)
ddx: Hepatic metastases, Cholangiocarcinoma, Hepatic adenoma, FNH, Hemangioma, Regenerative/dysplastic nodule
labs:
Ultrasound q6 months (surveillance in cirrhotics)
AFP (elevated but nonspecific)
Triple-phase CT or MRI with contrast (arterial enhancement with washout = diagnostic)
LI-RADS classification
LFTs, AFP, MELD score
Hepatitis B/C serologies
Biopsy only if imaging inconclusive (risk of seeding)
Chest CT (staging)
notes:
Leading cause of cancer death worldwide
Surveillance: US + AFP q6 months in cirrhosis patients (any etiology)
Diagnosis often by imaging alone (no biopsy needed if classic enhancement pattern on CT/MRI)
Milan criteria for transplant: single ≤5cm or 2-3 nodules each ≤3cm, no vascular invasion, no extrahepatic disease
BCLC staging guides treatment: 0/A (curative), B (TACE), C (systemic), D (palliative)
AFP >400 highly suggestive but only 50% sensitive
Hep B: HCC can occur WITHOUT cirrhosis (screen HBV carriers regardless)
Risk: HCV cirrhosis (1-4%/year), HBV, alcohol, NASH cirrhosis (increasing)
description: The most common primary liver cancer, usually developing in the setting of chronic liver disease or cirrhosis, with surveillance enabling early detection and potentially curative treatment.

name: Gastric Cancer
signs:
Epigastric pain
Weight loss
Early satiety
Nausea/vomiting
Dysphagia (proximal tumors)
Melena or hematemesis
Virchow node (left supraclavicular)
Sister Mary Joseph nodule (periumbilical)
Blumer shelf (palpable rectal shelf)
Krukenberg tumor (ovarian metastasis)
treatment:
1. Surgical resection (subtotal or total gastrectomy) for localized disease
2. D2 lymph node dissection
3. Perioperative chemo (FLOT regimen) for locally advanced
4. Adjuvant chemoradiation (MacDonald regimen, alternative)
5. Metastatic: systemic therapy based on molecular profile
6. HER2+: trastuzumab + chemotherapy
7. PD-L1+/MSI-H: immunotherapy
meds:
FLOT: 5-FU + leucovorin + oxaliplatin + docetaxel (perioperative)
Capecitabine + oxaliplatin (XELOX)
Trastuzumab 6mg/kg IV q3wk (HER2+ metastatic)
Pembrolizumab (PD-L1 CPS ≥1 or MSI-H)
Nivolumab (adjuvant after neoadjuvant chemo + surgery)
Ramucirumab + paclitaxel (second-line)
Trastuzumab deruxtecan (HER2+ second-line)
ddx: Peptic ulcer disease, GERD, Functional dyspepsia, Gastric lymphoma (MALT), GIST, Pancreatic cancer, Gastric polyps
labs:
EGD with multiple biopsies (diagnostic)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (staging)
EUS (T and N staging)
PET-CT (metastatic workup)
CBC (anemia)
LFTs, albumin
HER2 testing (IHC/FISH)
PD-L1 CPS
MSI/dMMR testing
CEA, CA 19-9
H. pylori testing
notes:
Leading cause of cancer death globally (especially Asia, South America)
Intestinal type: better prognosis, associated with H. pylori, atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia
Diffuse type: worse prognosis, linitis plastica (leather bottle stomach), signet ring cells
H. pylori: WHO class I carcinogen, eradication reduces risk
Hereditary diffuse gastric cancer: CDH1 mutation, prophylactic gastrectomy recommended
Classic metastatic signs: Virchow (left supraclavicular), Krukenberg (ovarian), Sister Mary Joseph (umbilical)
Screening in high-risk countries (Japan, Korea): regular EGD detects early-stage disease
description: Cancer of the stomach lining, often diagnosed at advanced stages in Western countries, strongly associated with H. pylori infection and treated with surgery and chemotherapy.

name: Ovarian Cancer
signs:
Often asymptomatic until advanced (silent killer)
Abdominal bloating and distension
Pelvic/abdominal pain
Early satiety
Urinary frequency
Ascites
Weight loss
Palpable adnexal mass
Bowel obstruction (advanced)
treatment:
1. Surgical staging and debulking (goal: no visible residual disease)
2. Total abdominal hysterectomy + bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy + omentectomy
3. Adjuvant carboplatin + paclitaxel x6 cycles
4. PARP inhibitor maintenance (BRCA-mutated or HRD+)
5. Bevacizumab (advanced disease)
6. Neoadjuvant chemo if unresectable at presentation
7. Genetic testing for all epithelial ovarian cancer
meds:
Carboplatin AUC 5-6 + paclitaxel 175mg/m2 IV q3wk x6
Olaparib 300mg BID (PARP inhibitor, BRCA+ maintenance)
Niraparib 200-300mg daily (maintenance, all comers)
Bevacizumab 15mg/kg IV q3wk
Pegylated liposomal doxorubicin (recurrent)
Gemcitabine + carboplatin (platinum-sensitive recurrence)
ddx: Ovarian cyst, Endometriosis, Uterine fibroids, Tubo-ovarian abscess, GI malignancy (peritoneal carcinomatosis), Primary peritoneal cancer, Fallopian tube cancer
labs:
CA-125 (elevated in 80% of advanced, not screening in general population)
Pelvic ultrasound (complex adnexal mass)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (staging)
Paracentesis with cytology (if ascites)
CBC, BMP, LFTs
HE4 (supplementary marker)
Risk of Malignancy Index (RMI)
BRCA1/2 and HRD testing (all patients)
Genetic counseling and panel testing
notes:
5th leading cause of cancer death in women, most lethal gynecologic malignancy
70% diagnosed at stage III-IV (lack of effective screening)
Epithelial (90%): high-grade serous most common subtype
BRCA1/2 mutations: 15-20% of epithelial ovarian cancers (PARP inhibitor eligibility)
Risk-reducing BSO recommended for BRCA carriers (by age 35-40 for BRCA1, 40-45 for BRCA2)
CA-125: not recommended for screening (poor specificity), useful for monitoring treatment response
Platinum-free interval: >6 months = platinum-sensitive (better prognosis)
Optimal debulking (<1cm residual) = most important surgical prognostic factor
description: Cancer originating in the ovaries, often called the 'silent killer' because it frequently goes undetected until advanced stages, with BRCA gene mutations significantly increasing risk.
